raw_case:
Reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome secondary to chronic cocaine abuse: Case report
We describe the case of a 36-year-old male patient with a history of chronic headache who came to the emergency room due to an intense global headache, associated with a tonic-clonic episode and loss of consciousness. A cranial computed tomography revealed subarachnoid hemorrhage without apparent aneurysms and a left temporal hyperdense lesion suggestive of intraparenchymal hematoma on the follow-up computed tomography. Cerebral angiography showed irregularities in the right internal carotid artery, including a pseudoaneurysm and multiple stenoses in pial arteries, which were related to chronic cocaine use. The final diagnosis was reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome, a rare but potentially serious condition, triggered in this case by cocaine use. Early diagnosis, through imaging studies such as cerebral angiography, is crucial to prevent serious complications like hemorrhages or ischemic events. This case highlights the importance of imaging in the diagnosis of reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome and its relationship with triggers such as sympathomimetic drugs.

Introduction

Cocaine is a widely used sympathomimetic drug worldwide, and its production and consumption have increased in recent years, predisposing individuals to the development of multiple pathologies associated with this practice. One of them is reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome, characterized by stenosis caused by cerebral arterial vasoconstriction. The development of imaging techniques has improved the diagnosis of this condition, allowing for appropriate approaches to its management.

Case description

A 36-year-old male patient with a history of chronic headache presented to the emergency department due to worsening symptoms, including a global headache, with an intensity of 10/10, associated with a tonic-clonic episode in the right upper limb, blurred vision, and loss of consciousness, without sphincter relaxation in the postictal state. On initial physical examination, he was alert, hypertensive, and showed no signs of neurological focalization. A noncontrast cranial CT scan revealed Fisher II subarachnoid hemorrhage, without apparent related aneurysmal dilations. A follow-up CT showed a left basal temporal subcortical intra-axial supratentorial lesion, hyperdense, likely related to an intraparenchymal hematoma with a hypodense halo suggesting perilesional vasogenic edema (Figs. 1A and B).Subsequently a cerebral panangiography was performed to rule out vasculitis or any arteriovenous malformation, identifying irregularities in the contours of the right cervical internal carotid artery, with a 5 mm lateral pseudoaneurysm and slight distal stenosis. (Fig. 2). Additionally, multiple areas of stenosis were observed in the medium and small caliber pial arteries across all vascular territories, as well as an old dissection of the right cervical internal carotid artery (Fig. 3). Autoimmune pathology was ruled out with normal extractable nuclear antigen antibodies (ENA) and complement levels. The findings were associated with chronic cocaine use, which the patient initially omitted from his history upon admission.

Discussion

Reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome (RCVS) is a set of rare and difficult-to-diagnose disorders resulting from multifactorial cerebrovascular dysregulation. There is no precise epidemiological data regarding its frequency; however, some reports show a highly variable incidence, reaching up to 50%, which has increased thanks to the growing understanding of this condition and the support of diagnostic imaging that confirms the findings [1]. It has been described to occur more frequently in women than in men, with a 2:1 ratio, increasing to 10:1 in the postpartum period [2]. The average age of onset is usually around the fifth decade of life, although cases have been reported in childhood, where it affects males more frequently [3].Regarding the pathophysiology, no single cause has been established for this syndrome. Some proposed mechanisms involve factors such as oxidative stress or endothelial dysfunction caused by multiple agents as contributors to the disease. Cocaine is a sympathomimetic drug; according to Colombian statistics, in 2019, the prevalence of consumption was 2.1% in the population aged 12 to 65 years [4]. Globally, the World Health Organization has reported an increase in production, with cocaine available in up to 90% of countries worldwide. Since 2011, its use in Europe has risen by up to 80% [5]. The active metabolite is benzoylecgonine, a sodium channel blocker that increases sympathetic activity at low doses. In the central nervous system, it blocks presynaptic nerve terminals and inhibits the reuptake of norepinephrine, serotonin, and dopamine [6]. There is an elevation of endothelin-1 in response to stress reducing nitric oxide production and causing sudden vasoconstriction. Additionally, increased dopamine levels trigger cerebral vasospasm.The most common symptoms are related to the criteria proposed by Singhal et al., showing a specificity of more than 98% and a positive predictive value (PPV) of 98%-100% for suspecting and differentiating it from primary angiitis of the central nervous system (PACNS). These criteria include: recurrent thunderclap headaches, defined as a sudden, global headache that reaches its peak intensity within 60 seconds and may be accompanied by vomiting, neck stiffness, and even altered consciousness of variable duration; or a single thunderclap headache with normal neuroimaging or watershed infarction/vasogenic edema; or no thunderclap headache but abnormal angiographic findings with normal neuroimaging. In primary angiitis, there are always abnormal imaging findings [7].Vasospasm and prolonged vasoconstriction can trigger ischemic strokes due to reduced arterial flow, as well as subarachnoid hemorrhage resulting from arterial wall rupture caused by increased intravascular pressure from vasoconstriction. Hemorrhage occurs less frequently, in up to 20% of cases [8].Approximately 70% of patients during the first episode of the syndrome present with neuroimaging that shows no pathological findings. However, after 2 weeks, 70% of those who require hospitalization will experience complications, starting with parenchymal or subarachnoid hemorrhages before cerebral ischemic manifestations. The gold standard for diagnosing this condition is cerebral angiography, where the most common finding is the “string-of-beads” sign, characterized by arterial segments alternating between dilated and narrower segments due to vasoconstriction, primarily affecting medium and large-caliber arteries. This finding tends to normalize within 8 to 12 weeks [9]. Other diagnostic methods such as CT and magnetic resonance imaging are highly useful for evaluating early signs of potential complications. Follow-up is preferably done with transcranial Doppler, a safe, noninvasive, and readily available technique for patients (Table 1) [10].RCVS has replaced terms for conditions now recognized as subtypes of this syndrome, each characterized by different triggers, such as postpartum cerebral angiopathy, since pregnancy is a prothrombotic state that alters vascular tone. Similarly, migraine-related vasospasm is considered another subtype, though it is not yet fully understood whether it is a risk factor for the syndrome or a manifestation of it. There are also numerous differential diagnoses for this condition, including intracranial atherosclerosis, moyamoya disease, and infectious vasculitis, among others. The condition most often confused with RCVS is PACNS; however, there are imaging characteristics that can help guide the diagnosis (Table 2) [11].

Conclusion

The advancement of diagnostic imaging, such as cerebral angiography, and the understanding of the pathophysiology of RCVS have allowed us to improve the timely and effective diagnosis of patients. Radiological signs are essential when there is a relevant clinical context, such as a history of cocaine use, to make the diagnosis.

Patient consent

Consent was received to publish both the clinical history data and the images from the patient. Available upon request.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 36-year-old male.
- **Chief Complaint:** Intense global headache.
- **History of Present Illness:** Chronic headaches with worsening symptoms, including a sudden, severe headache (intensity 10/10); tonic-clonic seizure in the right upper limb; blurred vision; and temporary loss of consciousness.
- **Past Medical History:** Chronic headache.
- **Personal History:** Chronic cocaine use (initially withheld information).
- **Family History:** None provided.
- **Physical Examination:** Patient was alert and hypertensive, with no signs of neurological focalization.
- **Ancillary Tests:**
  - **Initial CT Scan:** Fisher II subarachnoid hemorrhage without aneurysms.
  - **Follow-up CT Scan:** Left basal temporal hyperdense lesion indicative of intraparenchymal hematoma and surrounding vasogenic edema.
  - **Cerebral Angiography:** Irregularities in the right internal carotid artery (pseudoaneurysm, old arterial dissection, and multiple pial arterial stenoses).
  - **Autoimmune Tests:** Normal ENA antibodies and complement levels.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Primary Considerations:** The patient's combination of severe headache, seizures, and hemorrhages raised suspicion for cerebrovascular conditions such as reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome (RCVS), primary angiitis of the central nervous system (PACNS), vasculitis, or intracranial vascular lesions (e.g., aneurysm or arteriovenous malformation).
   
2. **Exclusion of Intracranial Vascular Lesions:**
   - CT and angiography did not reveal aneurysms or arteriovenous malformations, ruling out these causes.

3. **Consideration of PACNS:**
   - PACNS typically presents with persistent angiographic abnormalities and is associated with autoimmune markers or cerebrospinal fluid abnormalities. Normal autoimmune test results and the transient nature of the angiographic findings in this case make PACNS less likely.

4. **Exclusion of Other Vasculitides:**
   - Autoimmune pathology and infectious vasculitis were ruled out through laboratory tests and angiographic findings inconsistent with these conditions.

5. **Confirmation of RCVS:**
   - The “string-of-beads” angiographic finding (alternating arterial stenoses and dilations), combined with a history of chronic cocaine use (a known trigger of RCVS), supported the diagnosis. The absence of thunderclap headaches, commonly seen in RCVS, did not preclude the diagnosis given the imaging and clinical context.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome (RCVS) secondary to chronic cocaine use. The diagnosis is supported by the patient’s clinical presentation, history of cocaine use, angiographic findings of reversible pial arterial stenoses, absence of autoimmune pathology, and correlation with known triggers of RCVS.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome (RCVS) secondary to chronic cocaine use.

- **Patient Information:** 36-year-old male.

  - **Cerebral Angiography:** Irregularities in the right internal carotid artery (pseudoaneurysm, old arterial dissection, and multiple pial arterial stenoses).

results.content:
<think>
Okay, let me try to work through this case step by step. The patient is a 36-year-old male with a history of chronic headaches that have worsened. He had a sudden severe headache rated 10/10, a tonic-clonic seizure in the right upper limb, blurred vision, and temporary loss of consciousness. His past medical history includes chronic headaches, but he initially didn't mention his chronic cocaine use. His physical exam shows he's alert and hypertensive, no focal neurological signs. The tests include a CT scan showing subarachnoid hemorrhage (Fisher II), then a follow-up CT with an intraparenchymal hematoma in the left basal temporal area and vasogenic edema. Cerebral angiography found irregularities in the right internal carotid artery: pseudoaneurysm, old dissection, and multiple pial stenoses. Autoimmune tests were normal.

First, the initial presentation of a sudden severe headache (thunderclap headache) is classic for subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH). The Fisher II classification on CT indicates SAH without aneurysms. But then the follow-up CT shows an intraparenchymal hematoma. That makes me think about possible causes of SAH leading to a secondary intracerebral bleed. The patient's cocaine use is a key factor here. Cocaine can cause vasospasm, arterial dissection, and even pseudoaneurysms. The angiography findings support this: pseudoaneurysm and old dissection in the right ICA. The dissection could have led to SAH initially, and then maybe a secondary hemorrhage into the parenchyma due to the pseudoaneurysm rupturing or another complication. The seizure and neurological symptoms could be from the intraparenchymal hematoma causing increased ICP or direct brain injury. The absence of focal signs on exam might be because the hematoma is in a location that's not causing obvious focal deficits, but the seizure suggests some irritation or involvement. The autoimmune tests are normal, so no autoimmune vasculitis to consider. The history of chronic headaches might be related to the underlying vascular issues from cocaine use. So putting it all together, the primary diagnosis is SAH due to cocaine-induced arterial dissection and pseudoaneurysm, leading to a secondary intraparenchymal hemorrhage. The final diagnosis would be subarachnoid hemorrhage with intraparenchymal hematoma secondary to cocaine-induced arterial dissection and pseudoaneurysm of the right internal carotid artery.
</think>

```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient's presentation of a sudden, severe headache (10/10) with loss of consciousness and seizure is highly suggestive of a central nervous system emergency, most commonly subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH) or intracerebral hemorrhage (ICH). The initial CT findings of Fisher II subarachnoid hemorrhage confirm SAH, while the follow-up CT reveals an intraparenchymal hematoma in the left basal temporal region, indicating secondary ICH.  
<step 2> The cerebral angiography findings of a pseudoaneurysm, old arterial dissection, and pial stenoses in the right internal carotid artery (ICA) point to a vascular etiology. These abnormalities are strongly associated with cocaine use, which is a known cause of vasculopathy, including arterial dissection and pseudoaneurysm formation.  
<step 3> The patient’s history of chronic cocaine use, initially withheld, is critical. Cocaine induces vasoconstriction, increases blood pressure, and predisposes to arterial wall injury, leading to dissection, aneurysm formation, and hemorrhage. The absence of focal neurological deficits on examination may reflect the acute phase of hemorrhage or the location of the intraparenchymal lesion.  
<step 4> Autoimmune tests were normal, ruling out vasculitis or other autoimmune causes of vascular injury. The chronic headaches likely represent prior episodes of vasospasm or microhemorrhages secondary to cocaine-induced vascular damage.  
<step 5> The combination of SAH, intraparenchymal hematoma, and vascular abnormalities (pseudoaneurysm, dissection) in the context of cocaine use confirms a diagnosis of hemorrhagic stroke secondary to cocaine-induced arterial pathology.  

### Answer:
Subarachnoid hemorrhage with intraparenchymal hematoma secondary to cocaine-induced right internal carotid artery dissection, pseudoaneurysm, and pial stenoses.  
```
